policy_summary: Alabama authorizes ADS-equipped vehicles under statewide rules and limits separate requirements from political subdivisions.

Alaska considered autonomous vehicle legislation during the 2025-2026 legislative session, but a settled comprehensive framework for broad commercial driverless operation was not identified in this preliminary review.
Statewide framework supports testing and commercial operation.
Arkansas permits autonomous and fully autonomous vehicles through a State Highway Commission-approved program. Local governments cannot impose separate AV requirements or taxes.
Commercial deployment is allowed through a more complex permit system.
Statewide law authorizes automated driving systems and limits separate local standards.

Connecticut primarily allows autonomous vehicle testing through a controlled pilot framework. Testers must register vehicles, and the framework does not broadly authorize commercial driverless deployment.

Delaware has an autonomous vehicle advisory council but no comprehensive enacted framework for broad driverless commercial deployment. Pending legislation would require a human safety operator in certain heavy autonomous vehicles.
Fully autonomous operation is permitted under statewide rules.
Georgia allows fully autonomous vehicles to operate without a human driver when federal safety, registration, insurance, and traffic-law requirements are satisfied.
Hawaii has deployed autonomous passenger shuttles in a limited airport setting, but no comprehensive statewide framework for broad public commercial driverless service was identified.
Idaho created a committee to study autonomous vehicle testing and deployment, but no comprehensive statewide framework for broad commercial driverless operation was identified in this review.

Illinois has considered several competing proposals covering autonomous vehicle testing, deployment, and restrictions. A settled comprehensive statewide commercial framework was not identified in this preliminary review.
Indiana does not currently allow fully autonomous vehicles to operate on public roads. Existing vehicle and traffic laws continue to assume the presence of a human driver.
Iowa authorizes driverless-capable vehicles under statewide Iowa DOT rules. Level 4 and Level 5 vehicles may operate without a conventional human driver.
Kansas permits driverless-capable vehicles and autonomous transportation networks under statewide rules. Operators must meet insurance, registration, and law-enforcement interaction requirements.
Kentucky permits fully autonomous operation and uses statewide rules. Vehicles over 62,000 pounds temporarily require a human driver through July 31, 2026.
Louisiana has statewide rules for autonomous commercial motor vehicles, including vehicles designed to operate without a driver.
Maine has a state AV pilot program that allows approved testing on public roads. It does not broadly authorize public commercial driverless service.
Maryland does not yet have a comprehensive enacted framework for broad commercial driverless operation. Legislation proposing such a framework was considered during the 2026 session.
Massachusetts permits approved testing of automated driving systems on public roads. MassDOT approval and designated human test drivers are required under the current testing process.
Michigan recognizes automated operation without a physically present human.
Minnesota does not currently have a comprehensive autonomous vehicle statute. Testing and operation must comply with existing traffic and vehicle laws.
Mississippi permits fully autonomous vehicles but requires registration, financial responsibility, and a law-enforcement interaction plan.
Missouri considered legislation in 2026 that would authorize fully autonomous vehicles and establish uniform statewide rules. The proposal is treated as pending until enactment is confirmed.

Montana restricts Level 4 and Level 5 automated driving systems unless operation is specifically authorized through state rulemaking. Broad commercial driverless deployment is not treated as generally authorized.
Nebraska authorizes driverless-capable vehicles and on-demand autonomous transportation networks under uniform statewide rules.
State law broadly permits automated vehicle operation.
New Hampshire has a statewide automated vehicle testing and deployment pilot program. Participation requires approval and does not amount to broad unrestricted commercial authorization.
New Jersey does not yet have a comprehensive enacted framework for broad commercial driverless operation. A pending bill would create a three-year fully autonomous vehicle pilot program.

New Mexico enacted a statewide autonomous vehicle framework. Testing requires advance notification to the transportation department, and local governments generally cannot prohibit AV testing or operation.
New York requires a DMV permit for autonomous vehicle testing or demonstrations. Broad public commercial driverless operation has not yet been authorized under the testing framework.
North Carolina permits fully autonomous vehicles and prevents local governments from creating separate AV operating rules.

North Dakota allows autonomous vehicles without human drivers and permits on-demand autonomous transportation networks. Local governments generally cannot impose AV-specific fees or operating requirements.
Ohio permits autonomous vehicle testing after registration with DriveOhio. The current framework primarily supports testing and pilot deployment rather than broad public commercial service.
Oklahoma permits fully autonomous operation under statewide rules and preempts separate county and municipal AV laws.

Oregon coordinates automated vehicle policy through ODOT. Its testing-notification process is voluntary and requests evidence of at least $5 million in financial responsibility, but the state does not broadly authorize commercial driverless service.
Pennsylvania allows highly automated vehicles to operate with or without an onboard human driver, subject to state certification and compliance requirements.
Rhode Island has conducted autonomous shuttle pilots, but has not enacted a comprehensive statewide framework for broad commercial driverless deployment.
South Carolina has considered autonomous vehicle legislation, but a current comprehensive statewide framework for broad commercial driverless operation was not identified in this preliminary review.
South Dakota permits fully autonomous vehicles to operate without a human driver and generally prevents state agencies or local governments from prohibiting their operation.
Tennessee permits ADS-operated vehicles without a human driver but requires substantial liability coverage.
Uniform statewide rules limit separate municipal AV regulation.
Utah has a statewide autonomous vehicle framework.
Vermont allows permitted AV testing, but requires an onboard human operator capable of immediately taking control and at least $5 million in liability coverage.
Virginia does not yet have a comprehensive statewide framework for driverless commercial deployment. A state work group is developing recommendations and draft legislation.

Washington operates an autonomous vehicle self-certification testing program. Participating entities must maintain at least $5 million in liability insurance, but the program does not provide broad commercial authorization.
West Virginia permits fully autonomous passenger and commercial vehicles. Local governments cannot impose separate AV-specific fees, taxes, performance standards, or operating restrictions.
Wisconsin currently requires an operator to remain behind the wheel and in physical control of the vehicle. Testing is allowed only when a responsible human operator is present.
Wyoming has a statewide framework regulating the registration, licensing, testing, and operation of vehicles equipped with automated driving systems.
